nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated. Generally reported from muddy and sandy bottoms adjacent to rocky structure and patch reefs, in coastal lagoons, seagrass beds, and inshore reef edges. Reported depth range 0–30 m (most records 1–15 m). Tends to shelter cryptically among seagrass, rubble, and rockwork during the day and is reported to use the spines of long-spined sea urchins as cover, foraging more actively at dusk and through the night.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine flesh, krill, copepods, amphipods, and quality prepared frozen carnivore foods. Pellet and flake foods tend to be accepted once specimens are settled, though newly imported individuals may initially refuse dry foods. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised, and dusk or evening feeding has been reported to improve acceptance given the species' nocturnal foraging tendency.

summary: The Speckled Fowler Cardinalfish (Fowleria isostigma) is a small, generally reef-safe Indo-Pacific species with tan, black, and green camouflage patterning. Peaceful and nocturnal, it shelters among live rock, seagrass, and urchin spines. An orange-red form sold under a similar name by some retailers appears to be a different species.
careNotes:
The Speckled Fowler Cardinalfish is a small Indo-Pacific member of the family Apogonidae in the genus Fowleria, typically reaching around 7–9 cm in length. The species (Fowleria isostigma) is identified by its distinctive tan, black, and green camouflage coloration with small dark spots on the scales and a characteristic black spot behind the gill plate.

A note on trade identification: some retailers list an orange-to-red cardinalfish with a red or yellow tail under the name 'Fowleria Cardinalfish' and assign it the same scientific name, F. isostigma. The coloration of that form is strikingly different from the tan/black cryptic patterning that characterizes this species, and it is likely a misidentification — possibly a different Fowleria species or an entirely distinct cardinalfish. Buyers seeking the speckled camouflage form should confirm the coloration before purchase.

In the aquarium the Speckled Fowler Cardinalfish tends to do best when given ample live rock with caves, overhangs, and rubble, along with seagrass or plant cover and dim shaded retreats. It is generally reported as reef-safe and has not been documented to nip corals or sessile invertebrates; very small ornamental shrimp may occasionally be taken, as with most marine carnivores.

The species is largely nocturnal in its foraging habits, becoming more active at dusk and through the night. Newly imported specimens may initially refuse prepared foods; live or vitamin-enriched frozen foods offered at dusk are generally reported to improve acceptance. Once settled, the species is commonly considered hardy provided water quality remains stable and feeding is consistent.
breedingNotes: Like other cardinalfishes, members of the genus Fowleria are paternal mouthbrooders, meaning the male holds and protects the fertilized egg mass inside his mouth until the larvae hatch. Pairs form during courtship, and after spawning the male is generally reported to carry the eggs in his buccal cavity (mouth pouch) for roughly 1–3 weeks while largely ceasing to feed; a visibly swollen jaw is the most obvious sign of active brooding. Documented captive-breeding records for Fowleria isostigma are sparse, and larval survival in a community tank is unlikely without a dedicated rearing setup.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Gave trade name 'Speckled Fowler Cardinalfish', scientific name as 'Fowleria sp.' (no epithet committed), origin Cebu/Indonesia, max size 3 in (~7.6 cm), minimum tank 30 gal (~113 L), care level easy, temperament peaceful, reef-safe yes, diet (meaty foods: feeder shrimp, flake, pellet, marine flesh, bloodworms), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), color form (black/tan/green with camouflage markings, black spot behind gill plate), behaviour (slow methodical swimmer, hides in seagrass or among long-spined urchin spines, small groups establish hierarchy without aggression).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Designated secondary source. The mnhn.fr mirror returned HTTP 403; data was obtained from the fishbase.se mirror for the same species page. Gave taxonomy (Apogonidae, Kurtiformes), authorship (Jordan & Seale, 1906), max size 7.0 cm SL, depth range 0–30 m, Indo-Pacific distribution (35°N–20°S, 109°E–179°E), habitat (muddy bottoms and reef-associated, found near rocky areas), wild temperature 25.8–29.3°C (mean 28.7°C, environmental modelling), reproduction mode (distinct pairing during courtship and spawning), and IUCN status (Least Concern, assessed 2023-08-14). Coloration: brown to tan body with small black spots on scales — consistent with the LiveAquaria 'Speckled Fowler' camouflage description.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (7 cm / 2.76 in), care level (average), minimum tank size (33 gal / ~150 L), temperature 24–27°C, depth 0–30 m, diet (brine shrimp, flakes, mysis, zooplankton), distribution (Indo-Pacific including Australia, Coral Sea, Philippines, Japan, PNG, Micronesia).

additional.name: Tank Facts
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.5 in / ~9 cm), temperament (peaceful, social, may become territorial toward conspecifics in tight quarters), diet (frozen and live brine, mysis, krill, marine pellet/flake), water parameters (76–80°F / ~24.4–26.7°C, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, dKH 8–12), and behaviour (nocturnal, generally hardy).

additional.name: Fishes of Australia
additional.notes: Cross-checked Australian distribution (Cocos Keeling, Ashmore & Cartier Islands, Scott Reef, Darwin, Cape York to Capricorn Group / Great Barrier Reef), habitat (reefs, muddy bottoms), max size 7 cm DL, depth 1–15 m. Confirms the Indo-Pacific reef-associated muddy-bottom habitat described elsewhere.

fish.breedingNotes: The Speckled Fowler Cardinalfish is a paternal mouthbrooder, as is typical of family Apogonidae. Pairs form during courtship and spawning, after which the male is generally reported to carry the egg mass in the buccal cavity (mouth pouch) for roughly 1–3 weeks and to largely stop feeding while brooding. Documented captive-breeding records for Fowleria isostigma are sparse, and community-tank larval survival is unlikely without a dedicated rearing setup.
